name: Pony ORM (Python ORM with Generator-Based Query Syntax)

auth.notes: Database credentials configured in bind(); no library auth

performance.notes: Identity map and unit-of-work; competitive with SQLAlchemy for typical OLTP

agent_readiness.notes: ORM with unique Python generator-based query syntax. from pony.orm import *. db = Database(). class User(db.Entity): name = Required(str). age = Optional(int). orders = Set('Order'). db.bind(provider='sqlite', filename='db.sqlite', create_db=True). db.generate_mapping(create_tables=True). with db_session: User(name='Alice', age=30). users = select(u for u in User if u.age > 25). Generator syntax translates to SQL. Supports: SQLite, PostgreSQL, MySQL, Oracle, CockroachDB. Magic: Python generator → SQL without explicit query objects.

gotchas: Generator query syntax is unique — requires learning new mental model
Less ecosystem integration than SQLAlchemy (fewer extensions, tools)
Metaprogramming magic can make debugging query translation difficult
Smaller community and fewer resources than SQLAlchemy/Django ORM
best_when: When Pythonic generator-based query syntax is preferred over explicit query builders
Small to medium applications where SQLAlchemy complexity is overkill
Prototyping where ergonomic ORM API matters
Applications where the unique syntax trade-off is acceptable
avoid_when: Large teams where SQLAlchemy is the organization standard
Complex query requirements needing low-level SQL control
When ecosystem integration with tools expecting SQLAlchemy is needed
summary: Pony ORM uses Python generator syntax for database queries — select(u for u in User if u.age > 25). Unique approach: Python AST parsed to SQL. Entity/Required/Optional/Set declarative model. db_session context manager. Supports SQLite, PostgreSQL, MySQL, Oracle. Apache-2.0. No async support. Most loved feature is the generator query syntax. Smaller ecosystem than SQLAlchemy. Best for developers who prefer this ergonomic query style over query builder APIs.
